metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact gate-membership predicate used in `calibrate_criterion`'s branch guard (e.g. 'call `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")` and branch on non-None return') so the executor has an unambiguous implementation target.
evidence: 'add a code-review branch that resolves the physical prompt id via criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review") — ... — when the id belongs to the code-review gate'
No predicate definition is given for the gate-membership test that selects the branch.
findings.finding: The gate-membership predicate for the new code-review branch in `calibrate_criterion` is unspecified. The plan says 'when the id belongs to the code-review gate' but does not define the detection logic — leaving the executor to choose between (a) checking registry membership, (b) matching a logical-id prefix, or (c) testing whether `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review")` returns non-None. These have different semantics for unknown/inactive ids.
findings.location: Approach section — `eval.py calibrate_criterion` bullet, phrase 'when the id belongs to the code-review gate'
scenarios: An executor choosing predicate (b) (prefix match on `project.`) would silently fail to route a future built-in code-review criterion that lacks the prefix; predicate (a) would reject inactive-but-valid built-in code-review ids; without a stated default the executor makes an arbitrary choice.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'Branch on the code-review arm when `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")` returns a non-None physical id; fall through to the existing plan-review arm otherwise.' This makes the predicate self-consistent with the resolver already in scope and avoids a separate registry lookup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly include the `run_case` call-site at `eval_solver.py:279` in scope, updating it to `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root=repo_root)` so `repo_root` is correctly threaded when `run_case` dispatches to the code-review arm.
evidence: Plan says 'Thread `repo_root` (already available and passed to `_run_code_review_case` at `eval_solver.py:204-234`)' but names only `_code_review_prompt_id` in the Scope bullet, not the call-site at line 279.
Current call: `if _code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id) is not None:` — no `repo_root` argument.
Scope bullets list `eval_solver.py — _code_review_prompt_id repo_root/gate-aware admission` with no mention of the `run_case` call-site update.
findings.finding: `_code_review_prompt_id` needs `repo_root` threaded through its call-site in `run_case` (line 279), but the Scope section does not name that call-site update. Without it, the extended function receives `repo_root=None` even when `run_case` has a valid `repo_root`, so project physical ids are always rejected at the admission guard.
scenarios: Implementer extends `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root=None)` but doesn't update the call at line 279; `repo_root` is always `None`; `effective_criteria(None)` returns only built-in ids; `project.foo` is never admitted; the happy-path test fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet for `eval_solver.py`: 'also update the `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id)` call at `run_case:279` to pass `repo_root=repo_root`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact branch predicate in `calibrate_criterion` for the code-review gate (e.g. whether to check `criterion_id` against `effective_criteria(repo_root, gate_key='code_review')` or use a prefix rule), co-located with the description of the new branch.
evidence: Plan states: 'add a code-review branch that resolves the physical prompt id via criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review") when the id belongs to the code-review gate'
src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py:416-420 — existing plan-review branch uses a `startswith('plan-review-')` check; no analogous predicate is specified for code-review
ABSENCE: the plan never states the predicate (prefix check, registry lookup, or other) that routes into the new branch
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `calibrate_criterion` should add a code-review branch 'when the id belongs to the code-review gate', but provides no decision rule (predicate) for how gate membership is determined from the `criterion_id` argument. The existing plan-review branch uses a `startswith("plan-review-")` string prefix check; the plan does not say whether the code-review branch should check a `startswith("code-review-")` prefix, consult `effective_criteria(repo_root, gate_key="code_review")`, or use another predicate.
findings.location: Approach section — 'In eval.py calibrate_criterion, add a code-review branch'
scenarios: An implementer uses `startswith('code-review-')` — but a bare logical id like `project.foo` passed directly to `calibrate_criterion` would not match, silently falling through to the plan-review branch and producing a wrong `plan-review-project-foo` prompt id
An implementer uses a registry lookup — but the plan does not specify which registry function or what to do if the lookup itself fails

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the discriminator used in calibrate_criterion's new code-review branch to determine that a criterion_id belongs to the code-review gate (vs. the plan-review gate). 'When the id belongs to the code-review gate' is stated as the branch condition but the mechanism is left implicit — an implementer could use a heuristic (e.g., checking for a 'project.' prefix) that would mis-route a project plan-review criterion through the code-review arm if both gates have project criteria activated.
findings.location: Approach section — eval.py calibrate_criterion description; also AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Gate-membership detection in calibrate_criterion uses `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)` from `rebar.llm.code_review.registry` — the same call used in eval_solver.py — to decide whether criterion_id is a code-review id before taking the code-review branch; alternatively, resolve via `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")` and check whether the result starts with `"code-review-"`.' Either phrasing is sufficient; the key is that it mirrors the eval_solver.py discriminator for consistency.

findings.finding: The gate-detection predicate — the decision rule `calibrate_criterion` uses to decide whether a `criterion_id` belongs to the code-review gate vs. the plan-review gate — is never specified. The plan says 'when the id belongs to the code-review gate' but gives no concrete rule (registry lookup, prefix test, explicit flag, etc.).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit Scope/Approach note (e.g., 'the existing plan-review dispatch in `calibrate_criterion` is left unmodified') so AC2 has a corresponding described deliverable rather than testing an undescribed assumption.
evidence: AC2: 'A plan-review criterion's eval path is unchanged (parity); verified by the same -k eval selector asserting the plan-review branch still resolves plan-review-<id>.'
ABSENCE: Neither the Approach bullet ('In eval.py calibrate_criterion, add a code-review branch...') nor any Scope line names an explicit task to preserve or regression-test the plan-review branch.
findings.finding: AC2 ('A plan-review criterion's eval path is unchanged') is a non-regression criterion with no corresponding described deliverable or explicit work item in Approach/Scope. The Approach section describes only additive changes (adding a code-review branch) and implicitly assumes the plan-review path is untouched — but no Scope item or Approach bullet explicitly calls out 'preserve the plan-review branch unmodified.' This means AC2 measures an assumption rather than a described deliverable, creating a one-way gap under E1's 'every criterion maps to described work' check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; Approach section (calibrate_criterion bullet); Scope section
scenarios: An implementer could restructure calibrate_criterion's dispatch logic in a way that accidentally breaks the plan-review branch — no described work item (e.g., 'do not modify the existing plan-review dispatch') guards against this, so the AC2 assertion is the only safety net, untethered to any specified deliverable.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach bullet for `eval.py`, append: 'The existing plan-review dispatch remains unmodified; this change only adds the new else/elif branch for code-review ids.' Then in Scope, add a note: '`eval.py` — add code-review branch only; plan-review path is not touched.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review")` satisfies Rule-of-Three (>=3 call-sites); if not, replace it with an inline expression (`f'code-review-{cid.replace(".", "-")}'`) in both `_code_review_prompt_id` and `calibrate_criterion` to avoid a premature abstraction in `criteria.ids`.
evidence: criterion_prompt_id has exactly 1 definition (src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py:36) and 5 existing call-sites (eval.py:419, plan_review/registry.py:302, workflow/criterion_preview.py:487, overlay.py:140 comment, editor_assets/rebarProvider.jsx:1090) — none accept gate_key.
The gate_key variant would be called in exactly 2 places: eval.py calibrate_criterion code-review branch and eval_solver.py _code_review_prompt_id extension.
The code-review physical id formula is already used inline at eval_solver.py:199: `f'code-review-{oid}' for oid in OVERLAY_IDS` — same pattern, no helper needed.
For project criteria: `'code-review-' + cid.replace('.', '-')` is the complete mapping, identical in structure to the plan-review formula, expressible inline without a gate_key parameter.
findings.finding: The plan delegates the logical→physical id mapping for code-review criteria to a gate_key-parameterised `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review")` added by child 1, but this abstraction gains only 2 call-sites (eval.py and eval_solver.py) while the equivalent expression is already an inline one-liner in the codebase (`f"code-review-{oid}"` at eval_solver.py:199). Rule-of-Three is not satisfied for the gate_key overload.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Build the admissible physical set by mapping each logical id from `effective_criteria(repo_root)` through `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review")`'; also eval.py calibrate_criterion code-review branch
scenarios: If child 1 adds `gate_key` to `criterion_prompt_id` and this ticket consumes it, the abstraction exists solely for 2 call-sites — failing Rule-of-Three. If a third gate is never added, the parameter becomes dead complexity.
If child 1 is delayed or redesigned, this ticket's approach breaks at the dependency seam unnecessarily.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_code_review_prompt_id`, build the project physical set inline: `{'code-review-' + cid.replace('.', '-') for cid in effective_criteria(repo_root) if cid.startswith('project.')}`. In `calibrate_criterion`, resolve the code-review physical id inline: `prompt_id = 'code-review-' + criterion_id.replace('.', '-')`. This removes the need for a gate_key parameter on `criterion_prompt_id` entirely and keeps the mapping co-located with its 2 use-sites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the Approach section whether `calibrate_criterion`'s code-review branch reuses `_criterion_solver` (which already dispatches to `_run_code_review_case` via `eval_solver.run_case`) or requires separate solver wiring.
evidence: eval.py:429-430: the plan-review branch assigns solve = _criterion_solver(...) when solve is None; _criterion_solver calls eval_solver.run_case, which dispatches to _run_code_review_case via _code_review_prompt_id
The plan describes loading the eval spec but does not say whether the same _criterion_solver is used or a new one is wired for the code-review calibration arm
The plan's 'Rejected alternative' confirms reuse of the single solver, but the wiring between calibrate_criterion's new branch and eval_solver's code-review dispatch is not spelled out
findings.finding: The plan describes adding a code-review branch to `calibrate_criterion` but does not state which `solve` callable is used in that branch; the existing plan-review branch falls through to `_criterion_solver`, which calls `eval_solver.run_case` (dispatching to the code-review arm via `_code_review_prompt_id`). Without an explicit statement, it is ambiguous whether the code-review branch in `calibrate_criterion` reuses `_criterion_solver` or needs a separate solver.
findings.location: Approach section: 'In eval.py calibrate_criterion, add a code-review branch that resolves the physical prompt id via criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review") … then loads its eval spec through the existing load_eval_spec(prompt_id, repo_root=repo_root) path.'
scenarios: An implementer might wire the code-review branch to skip the solve callable entirely, only load the spec, and never actually run the cases — producing a silent gap.
Or they might instantiate a separate solve that bypasses eval_solver.run_case's code-review dispatch, breaking the intended single-solver reuse.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'The code-review branch passes the resolved physical prompt_id to the existing `_criterion_solver` (unchanged); `eval_solver.run_case` already dispatches `code-review-*` ids to `_run_code_review_case` — no separate solver is needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to the Approach section explaining why routing code-review calibration through the existing `run_case` dispatch (inline-diff input path, unified fire/no-fire loop, gate-keyed dispatch) is the right fit — not just that the rejected alternative was worse.
evidence: Rejected alternative: 'A separate code-review eval entrypoint — duplicates calibrate_criterion and its solver wiring; instead reuse the single solver, keyed on the resolved gate.'

No sentence explains the positive case for why the `run_case` dispatch architecture fits code-review calibration — e.g., that it reuses the same inline-diff input path, keeps the fire/no-fire scoring loop unchanged, and lets future gate arms be added without touching calibrate_criterion.
findings.finding: The plan states a negative rationale for the chosen approach (rejects a separate entrypoint because it 'duplicates') but provides no positive rationale explaining why routing code-review calibration through the existing `run_case` dispatch is the right architectural fit.
scenarios: A reviewer or implementer reading the plan cannot assess whether the chosen approach is sound on its own merits — only that one alternative was worse.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the output-vocabulary mapping rule for `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review")` inline in this plan (e.g. `project.foo` → `code-review-project-foo`), so the gate-keyed signature contract is self-contained without reading child 1.
evidence: Plan states: 'criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review") — the gate-keyed resolver added by child 1'
src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py:36 — current `criterion_prompt_id` has no `gate_key` parameter; its only mapping rule is `plan-review-` prefix
Child 1 ticket rebar:fc72-bf7e-7652-4228 is inaccessible; the mapping rule (code-review gate: `project.foo` → `code-review-project-foo`) appears nowhere in this plan's text
The plan's Approach section names the gate-keyed call twice without specifying its output-value vocabulary for the code-review gate
findings.finding: The gate-keyed `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review")` signature and its output-vocabulary mapping rule (e.g. `project.foo` → `code-review-project-foo` for the code-review gate) are never specified within this plan — they are deferred entirely to child 1, whose ticket is inaccessible. An implementer of this ticket cannot derive the mapping without reading child 1, making this plan non-self-contained for the central id-resolution protocol it specifies.
findings.location: Approach section — 'criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review")' and 'eval.py calibrate_criterion code-review branch'
scenarios: An implementer adds `gate_key` to `criterion_prompt_id` but maps `project.foo` → `plan-review-project-foo` (the existing rule), producing a wrong physical id for the code-review gate
Test AC3 asserts logical→physical mapping correctness but cannot be written without knowing the mapping rule
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the Approach section: 'For `gate_key="code_review"`, `criterion_prompt_id` returns `code-review-<criterion_id.replace(".", "-")>` (mirroring the plan-review rule with the `code-review-` prefix instead of `plan-review-`).'

findings.finding: The plan says `_code_review_prompt_id` should admit ids present in `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)`, but `effective_criteria` returns logical criterion ids (e.g. `project.foo`) while `_code_review_prompt_id` receives a `prompt_id` with the `code-review-` prefix (e.g. `code-review-project-foo`). The plan does not specify the reverse-mapping step needed to reconcile these two formats, analogous to the reverse-mapping already implemented for plan-review in `_criterion_id` (eval_solver.py:72-87).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the `eval_solver.py` bullet: strip the `code-review-` prefix, restore the `project-` → `project.` dot, and check membership in `effective_criteria(repo_root)` — parallel to the `_criterion_id` logic at lines 72-87.

findings.finding: `_code_review_prompt_id` is extended to check `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)`, but `effective_criteria()` returns LOGICAL criterion ids (e.g. `project.foo`), while `_code_review_prompt_id` receives PHYSICAL prompt ids (e.g. `code-review-project-foo`). The plan does not specify how the physical id is matched against the logical-id vocabulary, leaving an implicit vocabulary mismatch that would cause the guard to always return `None` for project criteria.
findings.location: Approach section — `eval_solver.py` bullet: 'consult `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)` … in addition to the static built-in set'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact return-value contract of the gate-keyed `criterion_prompt_id` that child 1 must deliver — concretely: `criterion_prompt_id("project.foo", gate_key="code_review")` → `"code-review-project-foo"` — so this ticket's implementation has an unambiguous interface to code against.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py:36-42: `def criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id: str) -> str` — no gate_key parameter exists
src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py:42: always returns `f"{PLAN_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX}{criterion_id.replace('.', '-')}"` — hardcoded plan-review prefix
Plan quote: 'criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review") — the gate-keyed resolver added by child 1 [rebar:fc72-bf7e-7652-4228]'
rebar show rebar:fc72-bf7e-7652-4228 returned 'not found' — child ticket unverifiable via tool
No search hit for 'gate_key' anywhere in src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py or for 'criterion_prompt_id.*gate_key' anywhere in the codebase
findings.finding: The plan's core mechanism — calling `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review")` to map logical ids to `code-review-project-<name>` physical ids — depends on a `gate_key` parameter that does not exist in the current `criterion_prompt_id` function. The current implementation at `src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py:36-42` has the signature `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id: str) -> str` with no `gate_key` parameter and always prepends `plan-review-`. The plan correctly states child 1 adds this, but never specifies the required contract of the gate-keyed variant (which prefix to use per gate, error behavior on unknown gate key, whether `gate_key` is keyword-only), leaving this ticket's implementation under-specified should child 1 differ from what this ticket assumes.
findings.location: Approach section: 'criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review")' and 'gate-keyed resolver added by child 1'
scenarios: Child 1 implements `gate_key` with a different parameter name, keyword-only vs positional distinction, or different prefix logic — this ticket's code silently calls the old signature and produces wrong physical ids.

Reviewer implementing this ticket infers the gate-keyed contract incorrectly (e.g., using `code_review` vs `code-review` as the prefix) because the plan never states what `criterion_prompt_id("project.foo", gate_key="code_review")` must return.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Child 1 must extend `criterion_prompt_id` to accept a keyword-only `gate_key: str = "plan_review"` parameter; when `gate_key="code_review"` the prefix is `"code-review-"` instead of `"plan-review-"`, so `criterion_prompt_id("project.foo", gate_key="code_review") == "code-review-project-foo"`. This ticket's `_code_review_prompt_id` extension relies on this exact return value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behaviour for `_code_review_prompt_id` (and the `calibrate_criterion` code-review branch) when `repo_root` is `None` and the presented physical id is not in the static built-in set — either raise a located error naming the criterion and requiring a repo root, or document that this case is deliberately out-of-scope.
evidence: Plan states: 'Thread repo_root (already available and passed to _run_code_review_case at eval_solver.py:204-234)'
src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py:179-184 — `effective_criteria(repo_root=None)` returns only packaged built-in ids
ABSENCE: no fallback or error is specified for the repo_root=None case when a project criterion physical id is presented
The edge-case test in Testing section covers 'no eval spec' but not 'no repo_root for a project criterion'
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what `_code_review_prompt_id` (or the new `calibrate_criterion` code-review branch) should do when `repo_root` is `None` — i.e., outside any project context. `effective_criteria(repo_root=None)` returns only packaged built-in ids, so a physical id for an activated project criterion (e.g. `code-review-project-foo`) would be silently rejected (returns `None`) with no error message naming the criterion or explaining the missing repo context.
findings.location: Approach section — '_code_review_prompt_id repo_root/gate-aware admission'; Testing section — 'Edge (missing spec)'
scenarios: A user runs `calibrate_criterion('project.foo', gate_key='code_review')` from a directory not under a rebar repo; `repo_root` resolves to None; `_code_review_prompt_id` silently returns None; `run_case` raises a generic `ValueError` for an unknown id rather than a located error naming the criterion and the missing repo

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify which ticket in the stack creates `tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py` (or declare this ticket creates it), and make that dependency explicit in the Scope/Testing sections.

scenarios: An implementer following the plan to 'extend' the named file cannot proceed without knowing which earlier ticket creates it, or whether they should create it themselves.
If the implementer instead extends test_eval_solver_project_criterion.py or test_code_review_evals.py, the AC pytest selector `-k eval` could select unintended tests.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a dependency on the ticket that creates tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py and name it explicitly, or (b) state in Scope that this ticket creates the file (not extends it), with a note that plan-review project-criterion tests are in test_eval_solver_project_criterion.py for parity reference.

If the child ships the parameter under a different name or with a different contract, both call sites in this ticket will silently produce wrong prompt ids.
findings.location: Approach section / eval_solver.py `_code_review_prompt_id` + eval.py `calibrate_criterion` code-review branch
scenarios: Child 1 ships `gate_key` as a keyword-only param named differently (e.g., `prefix_key`) — both call sites raise TypeError at runtime.
Child 1 ships the gate-keyed variant but the plan's tests are written against the wrong parameter name, causing the AC tests to pass against a stub but fail against the real implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line interface contract in the Approach section, e.g.: '`criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")` returns `"code-review-" + criterion_id.replace(".", "-")` for any criterion_id; raises ValueError for an unrecognised gate_key.' This pins the contract independently of the child ticket's availability and lets reviewers catch drift.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
